What Is the Purpose of Biofertilizers? Advantages & Applications Explained
==========================================================================

24 Jul 2023 • 

[![Image 2: Copy Link](https://roibiologicals.com//wp-content/themes/wl/assets/imgs/share/link.svg)](https://www.addtoany.com/add_to/copy_link?linkurl=https%3A%2F%2Froibiologicals.com%2Fblog%2Fwhat-is-the-purpose-of-biofertilizer-advantages-applications-explained&linkname=What%20Is%20the%20Purpose%20of%20Biofertilizers%3F%20Advantages%20%26%20Applications%20Explained "Copy Link")[![Image 3: LinkedIn](https://roibiologicals.com//wp-content/themes/wl/assets/imgs/share/linkedin.svg)](https://www.addtoany.com/add_to/linkedin?linkurl=https%3A%2F%2Froibiologicals.com%2Fblog%2Fwhat-is-the-purpose-of-biofertilizer-advantages-applications-explained&linkname=What%20Is%20the%20Purpose%20of%20Biofertilizers%3F%20Advantages%20%26%20Applications%20Explained "LinkedIn")[![Image 4: Twitter](https://roibiologicals.com//wp-content/themes/wl/assets/imgs/share/twitter.svg)](https://www.addtoany.com/add_to/twitter?linkurl=https%3A%2F%2Froibiologicals.com%2Fblog%2Fwhat-is-the-purpose-of-biofertilizer-advantages-applications-explained&linkname=What%20Is%20the%20Purpose%20of%20Biofertilizers%3F%20Advantages%20%26%20Applications%20Explained "Twitter")[![Image 5: Facebook](https://roibiologicals.com//wp-content/themes/wl/assets/imgs/share/facebook.svg)](https://www.addtoany.com/add_to/facebook?linkurl=https%3A%2F%2Froibiologicals.com%2Fblog%2Fwhat-is-the-purpose-of-biofertilizer-advantages-applications-explained&linkname=What%20Is%20the%20Purpose%20of%20Biofertilizers%3F%20Advantages%20%26%20Applications%20Explained "Facebook")[](https://www.addtoany.com/share)

![Image 6: What Is the Purpose of Biofertilizers | ROI Biologicals](https://roibiologicals.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/What-Is-the-Purpose-of-Biofertilizers.jpg)

![Image 7: What Is the Purpose of Biofertilizers | ROI Biologicals](https://roibiologicals.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/What-Is-the-Purpose-of-Biofertilizers.jpg)

Biofertilizers have gained significant attention in recent years due to their potential to enhance plant growth while promoting sustainable agriculture. Unlike chemical fertilizers, biofertilizers are derived from natural sources and contain living microorganisms that facilitate nutrient uptake and improve soil health. These innovations have allowed farmers to explore the purpose, composition, and advantages of biofertilizers over chemical fertilizers. It is also important to understand the proper applications of biofertilizers. At ROI Biologicals, we not only sell [high-quality biofertilizers](https://roibiologicals.com/products#biofertilizers) but are dedicated to helping farmers of all kinds move toward this more sustainable option.

****What Are Biofertilizers?****
--------------------------------

Biofertilizers, as substances containing living microorganisms, play a crucial role in promoting plant growth and enhancing soil fertility. These biological fertilizers utilize beneficial microorganisms to increase the supply or availability of primary nutrients to plants. There are three main types of biofertilizers: bacterial biofertilizers, fungal biofertilizers, and algal biofertilizers.

Bacterial biofertilizers, such as Rhizobium, Azospirillum, and Azotobacter, consist of bacteria that form symbiotic relationships with plants. They fix atmospheric nitrogen, providing a natural and sustainable source of this essential nutrient to the host plants. By utilizing bacterial biofertilizers, farmers can reduce their dependence on nitrogen-based chemical fertilizers and enhance the overall nitrogen cycle in the soil.

Fungal biofertilizers, like mycorrhizae and phosphate-solubilizing bacteria, contain fungi that aid in solubilizing phosphorus in the soil. These beneficial fungi establish symbiotic associations with plant roots, extending their reach and enhancing nutrient uptake, particularly phosphorus. By increasing the availability of phosphorus, fungal biofertilizers contribute to improved plant growth, especially in phosphorus-deficient soils.

Algal biofertilizers encompass algae, such as blue-green algae (BGA) and Azolla, which play a dual role in fixing atmospheric nitrogen and producing growth-promoting substances. Blue-green algae are capable of fixing nitrogen through a process called nitrogen fixation, converting atmospheric nitrogen into a form that plants can utilize. Azolla, a water fern that forms a symbiotic relationship with the bacterium Anabaena, also fixes atmospheric nitrogen and can be cultivated in ponds or tanks before being applied to the soil.

As the demand for sustainable agricultural practices grows, biofertilizers have become a promising alternative to chemical fertilizers. Farmers are increasingly adopting biofertilizers to promote eco-friendly farming methods and reduce their ecological footprint. The examples of biofertilizers mentioned earlier illustrate the diversity of microorganisms and plants that can be harnessed to enhance crop growth and soil health.

****Why Biofertilizers Matter****
---------------------------------

Chemical fertilizers have long been used in agriculture for their ability to provide essential nutrients quickly. However, their excessive and indiscriminate use has led to several challenges. Chemical fertilizers can degrade soil quality, disrupt microbial populations, contribute to water pollution, and cause long-term ecological imbalances. Moreover, the high cost and energy-intensive manufacturing processes associated with chemical fertilizers make them unsustainable in the long run. Biofertilizers offer a viable alternative that addresses these challenges by promoting sustainable agriculture practices.

****Advantages of Using Biofertilizers****
------------------------------------------

### ****Improved Soil Health****

Biofertilizers enhance soil structure, nutrient cycling, and microbial diversity, leading to improved soil health and fertility. They foster a symbiotic relationship with plants, facilitating nutrient absorption and increasing plant resistance to diseases and pests.

### ****Sustainable Agriculture****

Biofertilizers reduce dependence on synthetic chemicals and minimize environmental pollution. They contribute to sustainable agricultural practices by promoting natural nutrient cycling and reducing the negative impact on ecosystems.

### ****Cost-Effectiveness****

Biofertilizers offer long-term cost benefits by reducing the need for chemical fertilizers and other synthetic inputs. They improve soil fertility, leading to higher crop yields and decreased input costs.

### ****Enhanced Nutrient Availability****

The microorganisms in biofertilizers help fix atmospheric nitrogen, solubilize phosphorus, and enhance the availability of other essential nutrients to plants. This leads to improved nutrient uptake and overall plant growth.

### ****Reduced Environmental Impact****

Unlike chemical fertilizers that contribute to water pollution and greenhouse gas emissions, biofertilizers have minimal environmental impact. They support sustainable farming practices by promoting organic and eco-friendly agriculture.

****Applications of Biofertilizers****
--------------------------------------

Biofertilizers can be effectively applied in various agricultural practices, with varying benefits depending on the types of crops being grown and the goals of each farmer.

The primary application is for the improvement of crop production. Biofertilizers are suitable for a wide range of crops, including cereals, legumes, vegetables, and fruits. They can be used in both organic and conventional farming systems to improve crop productivity and quality. Additionally, biofertilizers play a crucial role in the production of high-quality fruits, flowers, and ornamental plants. They promote root development, enhance nutrient uptake, and improve overall plant health.

On a global level, biofertilizers aid in reforestation efforts and land reclamation projects by improving soil fertility and supporting the establishment of plant cover in degraded areas. Biofertilizers are essential components of sustainable farming practices, such as integrated nutrient management, organic farming, and precision agriculture. They contribute to long-term soil health and help maintain ecological balance, making them a great choice for any farmers concerned about the Earth.

****Biofertilizers from ROI Biologicals****
-------------------------------------------

At ROI Biologicals, we take pride in being a leading provider of eco-friendly biofertilizers and soil health solutions. Our product range is carefully designed to enhance soil health, increase crop yields, and reduce the dependency on synthetic chemicals.

All of our solutions are eco-friendly and derived from natural sources. Our biofertilizers are formulated with the environment in mind, ensuring minimal environmental impact and reducing reliance on chemical inputs. By embracing sustainable materials, we promote responsible agricultural practices that prioritize the health of our ecosystems.

Furthermore, we understand the importance of providing expert consultation and support to farmers. We offer [free consultations](https://roibiologicals.com/free-consultation/), providing farmers with guidance on improving soil health, enhancing productivity, and achieving higher crop yields. We believe in empowering farmers with the knowledge and tools they need to make informed decisions about soil management and the application of our biofertilizers.

At RIO Biologicals, we pride ourselves on providing customized solutions tailored to the specific needs of farmers. We consider factors such as soil type, crop type, and geographical location when developing biofertilizer recommendations. By offering personalized solutions, we ensure that our biofertilizers are optimized for maximum effectiveness in each unique agricultural context.

[Our biofertilizers](https://roibiologicals.com/blog/what-is-the-purpose-of-biofertilizer-advantages-applications-explained/#biofertilizers)have a proven track record of delivering successful results. Through scientific research, rigorous field trials, and positive customer testimonials, we have established a reputation for providing effective and reliable biofertilizers. Our products consistently lead to increased crop yields, improved plant health, and the promotion of sustainable agricultural practices.

Our commitment to environmental sustainability, expert consultation, customized solutions, and proven results make us the best choice for farmers seeking to improve soil health, boost crop yields, and embrace sustainable farming practices. Biofertilizers: A Nexus between soil fertility and crop productivity under abiotic stress
=========================================================================================

Abstract
--------

High food demand for the world's teeming population necessitates the intensification of crop production in modern [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which requires the extensive use of synthetic fertilizers for higher crop yield. The excessive use of [chemical fertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chemical-fertilizer "Learn more about chemical fertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), despite the high nutrients contents and ability to grow crops faster, discovered to be dangerous to the health and environment besides polluting the groundwater and atmosphere in the future. The alternative to these, [biofertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biofertilizer "Learn more about biofertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") arose today due to their attributes towards eco-friendly, cost-effective, and easy to apply in the agricultural field. Biofertilizers are a batch of diverse microorganisms, which can induce plant growth-promotion activities along with soil health, even under abiotic stress conditions. Biofertilizers maybe plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria, [arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/arbuscular-mycorrhizal-fungi "Learn more about arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and as well as the consortia of other beneficial microbes. Biofertilizers can sustain plant growth performance, even in a challenging environment. The performance of perfect-candidate-biofertilizer in the agricultural field depends on crop type, properties of inoculants, technical background, and environmental condition. Biofertilizers can, directly or indirectly, help in attaining food security compared to the harmful effect of chemical fertilizers. A direct mechanism of Biofertilizers refers to phyto-stimulation and nutrient mobility, while an indirect mechanism poses bio-control activity. Direct mechanisms involve phytohormone production and phosphate, potassium, zinc, etc. [solubilization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/solubilization "Learn more about solubilization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). While, indirect-mechanism is HCN production, [siderophore](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/siderophore "Learn more about siderophore from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") production, antibiotic production, etc. The present review elucidates the diversity of [microbial inoculants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-inoculant "Learn more about microbial inoculants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (biofertilizers), their impacts on agricultural production through rising soil fertility, and overall crop yield. In line with related literature worked out by different researchers.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000232)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000529)

Keywords
--------

Biofertilizers

Chemical fertilizers

Crop yield

Eco-friendly

Sustainable food security

1. Introduction
---------------

The sustainability of the agricultural sector is a clef for feeding the emerging population and economic exports of a country; therefore, the growth and survival of a nation indirectly depend on its [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Over the decades, various innovations had elevated by scientists to make the agricultural sector more efficient ([Ajmal et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0030)). The major constraint of crop production in the developing world is characterized by the inaccessibility of the essential plant nutrients due to lack of sufficient quantity and type of fertilizer ([Itelima et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0375)). Fertilizer is defined in many kinds of literature as any material often applied to the soils that provide one or more essential nutrients for plant growth and development ([Vanlauwe and Giller, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0805)). The use of chemical pesticides and [chemical fertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chemical-fertilizer "Learn more about chemical fertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") virtue of their potential for quick nutrients release and growing the plants more rapidly and efficiently served their purpose up to a point ([Sneha et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0720)). However, the continuous use of chemical fertilizers results in the deterioration of soil quality and gradual loss of soil fertility, which might further lead to the accumulation of [heavy metals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/heavy-metal "Learn more about heavy metals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in plant tissue, affecting the nutritional contents of the yield and edibility ([Farnia and Hasanpoor, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0260)).

Biofertilizer is the [microbial inoculants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-inoculant "Learn more about microbial inoculants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that contain the culture of dormant or live cells of the effective strains of N-fixing, P-solubilizing/mobilizing, K-solubilizing ([Itelima et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0375); [de Vives-Peris et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0195); [Fasusi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0270)). Microorganisms at their cellular level which is often applied to seeds, soils, or compost material to accelerate the [microbial activities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-activity "Learn more about microbial activities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by such organisms through their multiplication and enhance the nutrient's availability, which can be easily accessible by the plants ([Boraste et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0130)). The biological activity of microbial inoculants helps in mobilizing the availability of nutrients and recovery of nutrients, thereby improving the soil quality in general ([Upadhyay et al., 2012a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0780); [Upadhyay and Singh, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0765); [Yadav and Sarkar, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0850)). Biofertilizers have the potential to impede the nitrification process for a long period while improving the fertility status of the soil ([Sun et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0755); [Fasusi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0270)). They are among the vital constituents of Integrated nutrient management (INM) strategies for meeting both the soil's productivity and sustainability and at the same time keeping the environment safe, being pollution-free, economic and source of renewable nutrients to the plants to augment synthetic fertilizers in the sustainable production system ([Yadav and Sarkar, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0850)). [Panda (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0590) documented that the impact of bio-fertilizers for yield [improvement ranges](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/range-improvement "Learn more about improvement ranges from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") between 35% to 65%. However, to potentially exploit the benefits of biofertilizers based on the consistency of their performance. These require frequent researches in different dimensions due to the complex biological interaction among the host, other [rhizosphere](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rhizosphere "Learn more about rhizosphere from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[microflora](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microflora "Learn more about microflora from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and fauna as well as the environment ([Berg, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0085); [Panda, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0590)). The use of bio-fertilizers, therefore, supplies and enhance the productivity per area in a comparatively short time, consume smaller amounts of energy, reduces contamination of soil and water, increases soil fertility, and encourages antagonism and biological control of phytopathogenic organisms ([Sneha et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0720)). This review paper elucidates the recent updates of potential-biofertilizers in crop production due to an increasingly diverse biological activity for soil enrichment, and yields increased per unit area, including abiotic stress conditions.

2. Biofertilizer and food security
----------------------------------

The introduction of ‘Green Revolution’ technologies significantly brings a positive impact on agricultural production initially. However, excessive use of chemical fertilizers destroyed not only the texture and other physicochemical properties of the soil ([Ye et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0860)) but are also harmful to insects, worms, and other microorganisms in soils, which ultimately reduce both the quantity and quality of production ([Khandare et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0425); [Jaipunya, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0395)). Owing to the current rapid human population there was a huge demand for foods on the one hand, and on the other hand, farmers aspire for higher profit ([Popp et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0620)). As such, excessive use of synthetic chemical fertilizers and exploitation of unlimited underground water were adopted by the farmers ([Stefanakis et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0740)). A dramatic increase in production and crop yield associated with these inputs motivated the farmers towards their excessive use beyond the plants' consumption ([Liu et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0470)). As a consequence, this resulted in secondary effects on both the plant itself, soil, and environments like increased atmospheric temperature and unpredictable climatic change ([Raza et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0645)). Besides, the [soil organic matter](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-organic-matter "Learn more about soil organic matter from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be reduced due to [soil acidification](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-acidification "Learn more about soil acidification from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") caused by the excessive use of chemical fertilizers, which is a threat to plants' growth and development ([Naher et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0555)). Nevertheless, the issue of food security is related to balanced fertilization of agricultural soils that sustainably increased productivity while providing sufficient, safe, and nutritious food ([Stewart and Roberts, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0745)). All things being equal, using both organic and inorganic sources of fertilizers followed by practicing the 4R fertilizer management strategies i.e. (i) right fertilizer source, (ii) right rate of fertilizer use, (iii) right time and (iv) right target place ([Kumar et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0440)) will help ensure appropriate nutrients utilization and maximized productivity ([Naher et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0555); [Gao et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0280)). Hence, food demand for the vigorous emerging population can attain sustainably.

3. Biofertilizers; classification and mechanism of action
---------------------------------------------------------

Several inoculations and their interaction with crop plants have been exploiting for the production of biofertilizers [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#f0005) classified biofertilizers into different groups based on their nature and mode of action ([Itelima et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0375)). The [microbial inoculants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-inoculant "Learn more about microbial inoculants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") used in crop production such as nitrogen fixers, phosphorus solubilizers/mobilizers, potassium mobilizers, [PGPR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plant-growth-promoting-rhizobacteria "Learn more about PGPR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), etc. ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#f0005).) incorporated on a variety of soils and crop types, hence; the knowledge of the variety and abundance of microorganisms in biofertilizers is a key to potentially exploits their impact in [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), including under abiotic stress ([Sahu and Brahmaprakash, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0690); [Fasusi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0270)). The application of biofertilizers is quite significant for the nutrients enhancements and overall increase in plant production in the sight of INM with the added benefit of growing crops on the least fertile soil ([Panda, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0590)).

![Image 6: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666049021000396-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (572KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666049021000396-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (572KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666049021000396-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Schematic representation; Influence of [biofertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biofertilizer "Learn more about biofertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on plant growth-performance and soil health. [VAM=Vesicular–arbuscular mycorrhiza, ISP=Increase [soil porosity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-porosity "Learn more about soil porosity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ISA=Increase soil aggregation].

Based on the functional properties, biofertilizers can classify into various types such as Nitrogen Fixing Biofertilizer, Phosphate Biofertilizers, Biofertilizers for Micro-nutrients, and Plant Growth Promoting Rhizobacteria, etc. Nitrogen-Fixing biofertilizers raise the nitrogen level of soil by fixing atmospheric nitrogen and making it available to the plants. Examples: _[Azotobacter](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/azotobacter "Learn more about Azotobacter from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_, _[Nostoc](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nostoc "Learn more about Nostoc from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_, [Rhizobium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rhizobium "Learn more about Rhizobium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), _[Azospirillum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/azospirillum "Learn more about Azospirillum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ ([Itelima et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0375)). Phosphate Biofertilizers include phosphorous solubilizing biofertilizers (PSB) and phosphorus mobilizing biofertilizers (PMB). PSB solubilizes the insoluble phosphate from organic and inorganic phosphate sources. Examples include species of _Bacillus_, _[Pseudomonas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pseudomonas "Learn more about Pseudomonas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_, _[Penicillium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/penicillium "Learn more about Penicillium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_, _[Aspergillus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/aspergillus "Learn more about Aspergillus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _,_ etc. ([Etesami et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0255)). PMB transfer the phosphorus from the soil to the root cortex. Examples: [Arbuscular Mycorrhiza](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/arbuscular-mycorrhiza "Learn more about Arbuscular Mycorrhiza from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AM fungi). Biofertilizers for micro-nutrients include silicate and zinc solubilizers bacteria. These bacteria degrade silicates and aluminum silicates in soil. Example: _Bacillus_ sp. Plant Growth Promoting Rhizobacteria (PGPR) are the bacteria present in the rhizospheric region ([Upadhyay et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0795)). They promote plant growth by acting as Bioprotectants, Biostimulants, and by improving nutrient availability. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#t0005) highlights the various types of biofertilizers, their contributions to plant growth, and the crops that benefitted from the application of these biofertilizers. Mechanism of action refers to the biological and chemical process by which microorganisms contained in biofertilizers exert their effects applied to the plant's [rhizosphere](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rhizosphere "Learn more about rhizosphere from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Vejan et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0810)). The rhizosphere is a thin layer of soil surrounding the roots characterized by high levels of biochemical activities and comprised of plant, bacteria, fungi, and soil constituents ([Nihorimbere et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0565)). Moreover to these zones were found to contain microbial cells of more than 1010 per gram of root ([Egamberdieva et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0225)), and over 30,000 different bacterial species ([Mendes et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0515)). Interestingly, these interactions between plant-root and microbial populations have characterized as symbiosis. As the former access the unavailable nutrients elements into available form through decomposition, the later benefit from [root exudates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/root-exudate "Learn more about root exudates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as carbohydrate, proteins, sugars, vitamins, mucilage, [amino acids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/amino-acid "Learn more about amino acids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and organic acids ([Bonfante and Genre, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0125); [de Vives-Peris et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0195)), which modify biochemical properties of the rhizosphere through the application of these root exudates by acting as a messenger between the microbes and the plants ([Pii et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0615); [de Vives-Peris et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0195)).

Table 1. Types of biofertilizers, and its role in crop growth-performance.

| Type of biofertilizer | Contributions to plant growth | Beneficial Crops | References |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| _Rhizobium_ | Fixes 40-200 kg N/ha. Meets up to 80–90% of N crop requirement. Maintains soil fertility. Increases the yield by 10–30%. leaves residual N for the benefit of subsequent crops. | Leguminous crops such as; Cowpea, Gram, Pea, G/nut, Soybean, Berseem, Lucerna etc. | [Panda, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0590); [Mazid and Khan, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0505) |
| _Azotobacter_ | Fixes about 15-20 kg N/ha/yr. Supplies 20-40 mg N/ha. Promotion of growth substances such as Indole Acetic Acid, Giberellic acid etc. Results in 10–15% increase in crop yield. Biological control of plant diseases and destroy plant pathogens. Maintains the fertility status of the soil. | Mustard, Sunflower, Grapes, Sugarcane, Banana, Water melon, Papaya, Coconut, Plantation and Forest crops | [Das, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0185) |
| _Azospirillum_ | Fixes N up to 10-20 kg per hectare. Enhances the intake of both minerals and water. Increases roots developments. Improves the vegetative growth and yield of crops. | Maize, Rice, Wheat, Finger Millet, Sorghum, Bajra, Oil seeds | [Mazid and Khan, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0505); [Kumar et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666049021000396#bb0435) |
| _Azolla_ | Fixes up to 9

enriched_points.expanded_content: Bio-fertilizers decrease reliance on synthetic fertilizers, promoting natural nutrient availability while enhancing soil health and reducing environmental pollution.
By enriching soil microbiomes and fostering biodiversity, bio-fertilizers support eco-friendly agricultural methods, improving long-term soil fertility and resilience against environmental stress.
Bio-fertilizers enhance crop yield and quality, addressing the demand for nutritious food while ensuring sustainable agricultural practices to support growing populations.
